course_info.title: Multimedia in Education
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 1 hour per session

learning_objectives: Explain Mayer's cognitive theory of multimedia learning and its three assumptions
Apply the twelve principles of multimedia learning to evaluate instructional materials
Distinguish between multimedia that helps and multimedia that hinders learning
readings: Mayer, R. - Multimedia Learning (3rd ed.) Ch. 1-3
Clark, R. & Mayer, R. - e-Learning and the Science of Instruction Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction: Multimedia is not inherently good or bad for learning. Its effectiveness depends entirely on how it is designed. Richard Mayer's research program has produced the most robust set of evidence-based design principles in educational technology.
key_concepts: Dual channel assumption: separate visual and auditory processing
Limited capacity assumption: working memory handles about 4 chunks per channel
Active processing assumption: meaningful learning requires selecting, organizing, integrating
Twelve principles: coherence, signaling, redundancy, spatial/temporal contiguity, segmenting, pre-training, modality, multimedia, personalization, voice, image, embodiment
Boundary conditions: expertise reversal effect
lecture_notes.demonstration: Principle identification exercise: watch three instructional videos and identify which multimedia principles each follows or violates
setup_requirements: Three pre-selected instructional videos of varying quality

review.value: Eliminate extraneous material: interesting but irrelevant stories, background music, and decorative graphics all hurt learning by competing for limited working memory resources
Principles like redundancy and modality may reverse for expert learners who have well-developed schemas and can process redundant information without overload

learning_objectives: Apply visual hierarchy principles to instructional graphics
Design effective diagrams, charts, and infographics for learning
Use color, contrast, and typography to guide attention and reduce cognitive load
readings: Reynolds, G. - Presentation Zen Design Ch. 2-4
Tufte, E. - The Visual Display of Quantitative Information (selected pages)

lecture_notes.introduction: Visual design is not decoration. In educational materials, every visual element either supports or competes with learning. This session teaches the graphic design principles that matter most for instructional media.
key_concepts: Visual hierarchy: size, color, contrast, position guide the eye
Gestalt principles: proximity, similarity, closure, continuity
Typography for readability: font choice, line length, spacing
Color theory for accessibility: contrast ratios, color blindness considerations
Data visualization: choosing the right chart type for the data and message
lecture_notes.demonstration: Slide redesign lab: take five data-heavy slides and redesign them using visual hierarchy, Gestalt principles, and Tufte's data-ink ratio concept

review.value: Tufte's principle: maximize the proportion of ink used to display data; remove chart junk, unnecessary gridlines, 3D effects, and decorative elements
Elements placed close together are perceived as related; use spatial grouping to show relationships in instructional graphics without requiring labels

class_schedule.topic: Audio and Video Production for Education
learning_objectives: Plan and produce educational audio content (podcasts, narration)
Apply video production techniques for instructional screencasts and lectures
Optimize media length and pacing based on attention and retention research
readings: Guo, P. et al. - How Video Production Decisions Affect Student Engagement (edX study)

lecture_notes.introduction: Video is the dominant medium in online education, yet most educational videos violate basic principles of cognitive science and production quality. This session covers both the science and the craft of educational media production.
key_concepts: Optimal video length: 6-9 minutes for maximum engagement (Guo et al.)
Personalization principle: conversational style improves learning
Voice principle: human voice outperforms machine-generated voice
Signaling in video: visual cues, annotations, and highlighting
Audio quality: the most important production variable for perceived quality
lecture_notes.demonstration: Record and edit a 3-minute screencast tutorial: demonstrate screen capture, narration recording, and basic editing with annotations
setup_requirements: Screen recording software (OBS Studio or similar)
Video editing software (DaVinci Resolve or similar)

review.value: Shorter videos (< 6 min) have highest engagement; talking-head segments re-engage viewers; Khan Academy-style tablet drawing outperforms PowerPoint slides; speaking rate should be brisk
Studies show viewers will tolerate mediocre video quality but will abandon content with poor audio; invest in a decent microphone before a camera

learning_objectives: Design interactive multimedia learning objects using H5P
Apply the active processing assumption to create generative learning activities
Evaluate when interactivity adds value vs. when it adds unnecessary complexity
readings: Fiorella, L. & Mayer, R. - Learning as a Generative Activity Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Interactivity is not inherently beneficial. Clicking buttons does not equal learning. Effective interactive multimedia requires the learner to engage in generative cognitive processing: summarizing, explaining, mapping, drawing, or self-testing.
key_concepts: Behavioral interactivity vs. cognitive interactivity
Generative learning strategies: summarizing, mapping, drawing, explaining, self-testing
H5P: open-source interactive content creation (interactive video, branching scenarios, drag-and-drop)
Segmenting principle applied to interactive content
Learner control: when choice helps and when it hurts
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build an H5P interactive video: add embedded questions, branching paths, and summary screens to an existing educational video
setup_requirements: H5P.org account or H5P plugin in LMS
An educational video to make interactive
Learning objectives for the interactive layer

review.value: Clicking 'next' is behavioral; answering an embedded question that requires retrieval and application is cognitive. Only cognitive interactivity promotes learning.
Activities that require the learner to produce something (a summary, a concept map, a diagram) activate deeper processing than passive consumption

learning_objectives: Distinguish between decorative and instructional animation
Design educational simulations that promote conceptual understanding
Apply the pre-training principle to prepare learners for complex animations
readings: Plass, J. et al. - Toward a Taxonomy of Playful Learning

lecture_notes.introduction: Animation and simulation can make invisible processes visible and allow learners to manipulate variables in systems too dangerous, expensive, or slow to explore in real life. But they can also overwhelm working memory if not designed carefully.
key_concepts: When animation helps: dynamic processes, spatial relationships, procedural steps
When animation hurts: decorative motion, unsegmented continuous animation
Simulation design: fidelity, scaffolding, guided exploration
Pre-training: teach component names and behaviors before showing the full system
PhET simulations as exemplars: research-validated interactive science simulations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Explore and evaluate three PhET simulations: analyze how each uses scaffolding, learner control, and visual representation to promote understanding

review.value: Before presenting a complex animation of a system (e.g., how a car engine works), teach the names and characteristics of key components separately so the learner can allocate working memory to understanding the process rather than identifying parts
Higher fidelity is not always better; novice learners benefit from simplified representations that highlight key relationships without overwhelming detail

class_schedule.topic: Accessibility and Universal Design in Multimedia
learning_objectives: Apply WCAG 2.1 guidelines to multimedia learning materials
Design multimedia content that is accessible to learners with disabilities
Implement universal design for learning (UDL) principles in media creation
readings: CAST - Universal Design for Learning Guidelines 2.2

lecture_notes.introduction: Accessible design is not an accommodation bolted onto finished products. It is a design philosophy that benefits all learners. Captions help deaf students, but they also help students in noisy environments, non-native speakers, and anyone who processes text better than audio.
key_concepts: WCAG 2.1: perceivable, operable, understandable, robust
Captioning and audio description for video content
Alt text and long descriptions for complex images
Color contrast and font size for visual accessibility
UDL: multiple means of engagement, representation, action/expression
lecture_notes.demonstration: Accessibility audit: evaluate a multimedia learning module against WCAG 2.1 criteria and produce a remediation plan
setup_requirements: Screen reader software for testing (NVDA or VoiceOver)

review.value: Provide multiple means of engagement (why of learning), representation (what of learning), and action/expression (how of learning) — designing for the margins benefits the center
Section 508 (U.S. federal), ADA Title II/III (public institutions), and AODA (Ontario) require accessible digital content; non-compliance creates legal liability

quizzes.topic: Multimedia Learning Principles and Visual Design

questions.question: An e-learning course features narrated slides where each slide contains the full narration text, a relevant diagram, and a decorative background image of a classroom. Identify all multimedia principle violations and explain how to fix each one.
questions.answer: Violations: (1) Redundancy principle — full narration text duplicated on screen while being spoken overloads the visual channel; fix: remove the on-screen text or reduce it to key terms only. (2) Coherence principle — decorative classroom image is irrelevant to the content and competes for visual attention; fix: remove the background image. (3) Signaling principle is likely missing — with a complex diagram, learners need visual cues directing attention; fix: use highlighting, arrows, or progressive reveal. Fixed design: diagram with signaling cues, narration, and minimal on-screen text (key terms only), no decorative images.

rubric.key: Provides specific, actionable fixes for each violation

questions.question: Research by Guo et al. (2014) on edX video engagement found that shorter videos have higher completion rates. What is the recommended maximum video length, and what two production techniques did the study find most effective for maintaining engagement?
questions.answer: The recommended maximum video length is 6 minutes (with 6-9 minutes being acceptable); engagement drops significantly after 9 minutes. The two most effective production techniques: (1) Alternating between talking-head segments and screen content (slides, demos) rather than using slides alone, which re-engages viewer attention. (2) Khan Academy-style tablet drawing/writing over a digital surface, which outperformed PowerPoint slides for engagement because it provides a sense of immediacy and human presence.

rubric.key: Correct video length recommendation with specificity
Identifies tablet drawing / informal style technique

questions.question: Explain the difference between behavioral interactivity and cognitive interactivity in multimedia learning. Give one example of each in the context of an online science lesson.
questions.answer: Behavioral interactivity involves physical actions (clicking, scrolling, dragging) that do not necessarily require meaningful cognitive processing. Example: clicking 'next' to advance through slides of cell biology content. Cognitive interactivity requires the learner to engage in generative processing — selecting, organizing, or integrating information. Example: an embedded question after a cell division animation that asks the learner to predict what happens if a specific protein is missing, requiring them to apply the model they just observed.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between behavioral and cognitive interactivity
Accurate cognitive interactivity example showing generative processing

questions.question: A colleague argues that making all multimedia accessible is too time-consuming and that accommodations should only be provided when a student with a disability is enrolled. Using UDL principles, explain why this approach is problematic.
questions.answer: UDL argues that designing for accessibility from the start benefits all learners, not just those with identified disabilities. Retroactive accommodations are more expensive and lower quality than proactive design. Examples: captions benefit ESL students, students in noisy environments, and those who learn better through text; alt text forces clearer thinking about the purpose of each image; clear navigation helps all students, not just those using screen readers. Additionally, waiting until a student discloses a disability creates a reactive, unequal experience and may violate legal requirements (Section 508, ADA). Proactive UDL is more efficient, more equitable, and produces better learning experiences for everyone.

rubric.key: Provides specific examples of how accessibility features benefit all learners

major_assignments.title: Multimedia Learning Module Production
major_assignments.description: Design and produce a 15-20 minute multimedia learning module on a topic of your choice. The module must include: (1) an instructional video (max 6 minutes) with narration and visual signaling, (2) an interactive H5P learning object, (3) an infographic or explanatory diagram, and (4) a written design rationale documenting which multimedia principles you applied and why.
objectives: Apply multimedia learning principles to design decisions
Create accessible multimedia that meets WCAG 2.1 AA standards
Justify design decisions with reference to learning science research
deliverables: Infographic or explanatory diagram with alt text

criteria: Effective visual signaling and spatial contiguity
Aligned to a specific learning objective
Provides meaningful feedback on learner responses
Clear visual hierarchy and information organization
Effective use of color, contrast, and typography
Justifies design decisions with research references
Evidence-based design aligned to multimedia learning principles
Accessible content meeting WCAG 2.1 AA standards
Thoughtful design rationale grounded in research

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on multimedia learning principles and design

lab_assignments.description: Weekly production labs: video editing, graphic design, H5P creation, accessibility testing

midterm_examination.description: Covers multimedia learning theory, visual design, and video production principles

final_project.description: Comprehensive multimedia course module with peer evaluation and revision

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory. Hands-on production skills require in-class guidance and peer feedback. More than 2 missed labs will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 10% per day for up to 3 days. Production projects require timely submission for peer review cycles.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer feedback on media productions is required. All submitted work must be individually produced unless otherwise specified.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All media content must be original or properly licensed (Creative Commons). Stock media must be attributed. Violations result in a zero and academic integrity referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer capable of video editing (8GB RAM minimum). External microphone. Screen recording software. Access to Canva or similar design tool. H5P account.
